did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a deep learning model named SkinNet, which integrates Central Difference Convolution (CDC) and Selective Kernel (SK) Convolution layers within a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architecture. This model was trained on a dataset of 10,000 dermoscopic images to perform binary classification of skin lesions into malignant (melanoma) and benign categories, achieving a validation accuracy of 91.3% and precision of 93.6%.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a novel deep learning model named SkinNet for melanoma detection. It details the model architecture (integrating Selective Kernel and Central Difference Convolutions), describes the experimental setup (training on 10,000 images, 50 epochs, Adam optimizer), and reports specific performance metrics (91.3% accuracy, 93.6% precision). Although published in a conference proceedings (GITCON 2025), it contains original results and technical contributions rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Integrating Selective Kernel & Central Difference Convolutions for Melanoma Detection
journal: 2025 Global Conference on Information Technology and Communication Networks (GITCON)
brief_summary: This study proposes SkinNet, a lightweight dual-path Convolutional Neural Network integrating Selective Kernel and Central Difference Convolutions for binary classification of dermoscopic skin lesions into benign and malignant categories. The model was trained on a custom dataset of 10,000 labeled dermoscopic images, partitioned into 70% training, 10% validation, and 20% test sets, with data augmentation applied to enhance generalization. Evaluated on a test set of 2,000 images, SkinNet achieved a validation accuracy of 91.3%, precision of 93.6%, recall of 88.6%, and an AUC of 97.2%. The results demonstrate that the proposed architecture effectively balances diagnostic accuracy with computational efficiency, making it suitable for resource-constrained clinical environments.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study performs binary classification of skin lesions into malignant (melanoma) and benign categories using a custom dataset of 10,000 dermoscopic images labeled as benign and malignant.
The study performs binary classification of skin lesions into malignant (melanoma) and benign categories using a custom dataset of 10,000 dermoscopic images labeled as benign and malignant.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of dermoscopic skin lesions into malignant (melanoma) and benign categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the model performs 'binary classification' of skin lesions. The dataset is divided into two classes: malignant and benign. The model uses Binary Cross-Entropy Loss with sigmoid activation, which is standard for binary classification tasks. Performance metrics reported include accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, AUC, and MCC, all of which are standard for classification evaluation.

datasets_used.name: Melanoma Skin Cancer Dataset
datasets_used.description: A custom dataset of 10,000 dermoscopic images labeled as benign and malignant, used for binary classification of skin lesions.

use.training: Used for training the SkinNet model with data augmentation (rotation, flipping, color jittering).
use.validation: Used for validation during training to monitor performance and prevent overfitting.
use.testing: Used for final evaluation of the model's performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, AUC, MCC).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, AUC, MCC) on a test set.

Melanoma Skin Cancer Dataset.notes: The model was trained on 7,000 images and validated on 1,000 images. The reported metrics above are from the test set evaluation. The paper also notes an MCC of 0.8272 and AUC of 0.9726 in the text, and precision of 0.9366 and recall of 0.8860 in the discussion.

architecture_used.pretraining: None (custom lightweight CNN, no dependence on large pretrained models)

architecture_details.structure: Dual-path architecture integrating Selective Kernel (SK) Convolution and Central Difference Convolution (CDC)
architecture_details.stem_layer: 3x3 convolution kernel with stride 1, followed by Batch Normalization and ReLU activation
architecture_details.skconv_blocks: SKConv Blocks 1 & 2 (SK1-SK2) for dynamic receptive field adjustment
architecture_details.cdc_blocks: CDC Blocks 1 & 2 (CDC1-CDC2) for edge and texture enhancement
architecture_details.classifier_head: Fully connected layer with Dropout (0.3) and Sigmoid activation

preprocessing_techniques: Normalization based on RGB channel averages and standard deviations
Data augmentation including random horizontal flips, rotations (up to 20 degrees), zooming, and color jittering (brightness and contrast shifts)
Handling of wrong images by replacing them with blank RGB images of similar size

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied during training as part of PyTorch's transformation pipeline to enhance generalization.
Applied up to 20 degrees during training to handle class imbalance and mitigate overfitting.
Included minor brightness and contrast shifts during training to improve generalization.
Applied as a data augmentation technique to enhance generalization and mitigate overfitting due to dataset imbalance.

conclusions: The proposed SkinNet model, a lightweight dual-path Convolutional Neural Network integrating Selective Kernel (SK) and Central Difference Convolutions (CDC), effectively classifies dermoscopic skin lesions into benign and malignant categories.

SkinNet achieved a validation accuracy of 91.3%, precision of 93.6%, recall of 88.6%, F1 score of 91.06%, AUC of 97.2%, and MCC of 82.7% on a test set of 2,000 images from a custom dataset of 10,000 dermoscopic images.

The integration of SK and CDC layers allows the model to dynamically adjust receptive fields for lesion scale variations and enhance edge/texture sensitivity, improving the discrimination between benign and malignant lesions.

The model balances diagnostic accuracy with computational efficiency by avoiding dependence on large pretrained backbones, making it suitable for deployment in resource-constrained clinical environments such as rural screening centers or mobile devices.

Data augmentation techniques, including random horizontal flipping, rotation, zooming, and color jittering, were effective in enhancing generalization and mitigating overfitting on the custom dataset.

The study demonstrates that deep learning models can aid in early detection of melanoma, potentially improving patient outcomes in regions with restricted access to professional dermatological evaluation.
important_remarks: The study relies on a custom, proprietary dataset of 10,000 images rather than established public benchmarks (e.g., ISIC, HAM10000), which limits the ability to compare results with existing literature or assess generalizability to external populations.

There is no mention of external validation; the model was only evaluated on a held-out test set from the same custom dataset, raising concerns about overfitting to specific data characteristics or collection biases.

The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) for the performance metrics, making it difficult to assess the statistical robustness of the results.
The dataset composition (class balance) is not explicitly detailed, although data augmentation was used to handle 'class imbalance,' which may mask underlying biases in the training data.

The model was trained on a single high-end GPU (NVIDIA A100), and while claimed to be 'lightweight,' the actual inference latency or model size (parameters/FLOPs) is not reported, limiting the assessment of its true suitability for resource-constrained edge devices.
The paper lacks discussion on model interpretability or explainability (e.g., saliency maps), which is critical for clinical trust and adoption in dermatology.
No comparison is made against state-of-the-art models on standard public datasets, so the 'superior performance' claims are relative only to the baselines tested on the custom dataset.
